title: Revenirea la bunul simț bugetar
summary: The article defends Prime Minister Bolojan's budget adjustment policies against two types of criticism: those who say austerity is unnecessary and those who advocate for spending cuts paired with tax reductions. Using macroeconomic data from 2015-2024, the author argues that Romania's unsustainable growth model based on low taxes and high spending had to end, and that the current modest consumption reduction is a small price to pay compared to a Greek-style crisis.
talking_points: Romania's 2015-2024 economic model of low taxes and high spending created an unsustainable fiscal deficit reaching 9.3% of GDP in 2024
Nearly 90% of Romania's fiscal revenues cover only rigid expenditures (salaries, social assistance, and debt servicing), leaving almost no room for discretionary spending
Romania had among the lowest tax rates in the EU across consumption, labor, and property taxes, making revenue increases necessary
The cumulative individual consumption growth of 29.27% over 2015-2024 means the projected 0.1% decline in 2025 is a minimal correction, not a catastrophe
A purely expenditure-cutting approach without tax increases would be politically impossible and economically counterproductive
related_concepts: EU convergence and public service expectations
author: Victor Giosan
